questions.prompt:
Your DynamoDB table has 100 provisioned RCUs. You are performing strongly
consistent reads on items that are 8 KB each. You expected to handle
100 reads per second but are getting throttled at around 50 reads per
second. Why?
questions.explanation: One RCU provides one strongly consistent read of up to 4 KB per second. For items larger than 4 KB, additional RCUs are consumed proportionally. An 8 KB item uses 2 RCUs per strongly consistent read. Therefore 100 RCUs can only handle 50 reads per second of 8 KB items, not 100. To handle 100 reads per second, you would need 200 RCUs.

options.a: The table needs a GSI to handle the read load
options.b: Each 8 KB item consumes 2 RCUs because one RCU covers only 4 KB
options.c: Strongly consistent reads cost 4 RCUs per read
options.d: The partition key has too many distinct values

questions.prompt:
Complete the statement:

  One RCU = one strongly consistent read of up to ___ KB per second.
questions.explanation: One Read Capacity Unit provides one strongly consistent read per second for items up to 4 KB. For eventually consistent reads, one RCU provides two reads per second (each up to 4 KB). Items larger than 4 KB consume additional RCUs rounded up to the next 4 KB boundary.

questions.prompt: Match each DynamoDB capacity unit to what it provides.
questions.explanation: One RCU provides one strongly consistent 4 KB read per second, or two eventually consistent 4 KB reads per second. One WCU provides one 1 KB write per second. Transactional operations cost double — a transactional write consumes 2 WCUs per 1 KB, effectively halving throughput. These ratios are essential for calculating provisioned capacity correctly.

questions.prompt:
Your DynamoDB table has 1000 WCUs provisioned. The average item size is
only 500 bytes. On paper you should handle 1000 writes/second, but you
are getting ProvisionedThroughputExceededException. CloudWatch shows
total consumed WCUs are well below 1000. What is happening?
questions.explanation: DynamoDB distributes provisioned capacity evenly across partitions. If all writes target the same partition key, only one partition handles all the load while other partitions sit idle. This causes throttling even when total table capacity is sufficient. The fix is to design your partition key for even distribution of writes across many distinct values.

options.a: Items under 1 KB still consume 1 WCU each
options.b: All writes are targeting the same partition key causing a hot partition
options.c: The table needs to be switched to on-demand mode
options.d: DynamoDB reserves 30% of WCUs for internal operations

questions.prompt:
Complete the statement:

  One WCU = one write of up to ___ KB per second.
questions.explanation: One Write Capacity Unit provides one write per second for items up to 1 KB. Items larger than 1 KB consume additional WCUs rounded up to the next 1 KB boundary. For example, a 2.5 KB item consumes 3 WCUs per write. Transactional writes consume 2 WCUs per 1 KB item.

questions.prompt:
You use BatchGetItem to retrieve 150 items from a DynamoDB table. The
response contains only 100 items and no errors. What happened to the
remaining 50 items?
questions.explanation: BatchGetItem can retrieve a maximum of 100 items or 16 MB of data per call, whichever limit is reached first. If the request exceeds these limits, DynamoDB returns the items it could process and puts the remaining keys in the UnprocessedKeys field of the response. Your application must check for UnprocessedKeys and make additional BatchGetItem calls to retrieve them.

options.a: They were deleted between the request and response
options.b: BatchGetItem is limited to 100 items or 16 MB — remaining items are in UnprocessedKeys
options.c: DynamoDB silently drops items that exceed the partition throughput
options.d: The IAM policy only allows reading 100 items at a time

questions.prompt:
Your DynamoDB table is in on-demand capacity mode. The table normally
handles 500 requests per second. After a marketing event, traffic suddenly
spikes to 5000 requests per second. You start seeing throttling errors.
Why is on-demand mode throttling?
questions.explanation: On-demand mode automatically scales, but it can throttle if traffic increases to more than double the previous peak within 30 minutes. Going from 500 to 5000 requests per second (10x) exceeds this adaptive capacity buffer. On-demand tables accommodate traffic up to double the previous peak and continue scaling beyond that, but sudden extreme spikes can temporarily exceed the scaling speed.

options.a: On-demand mode has a hard limit of 1000 requests per second
options.b: On-demand can throttle if traffic exceeds double the previous peak within 30 minutes
options.c: On-demand mode requires a warm-up period of 24 hours
options.d: The table auto-scaled but the partition split has not completed

questions.prompt:
Complete the maximum number of items per BatchWriteItem call:

  Up to ___ items per call
questions.explanation: BatchWriteItem can process up to 25 put or delete requests in a single call, with a total payload limit of 16 MB and individual items limited to 400 KB each. If some items fail, they are returned in the UnprocessedItems field. Note that BatchWriteItem does not support update operations — only put and delete.

questions.prompt:
A user has an IAM policy that explicitly allows s3:GetObject on all
resources. However, the user still gets Access Denied when trying to
read from S3. The account is part of an AWS Organization, and the SCP
attached to the OU has an explicit Deny for s3:GetObject.
What takes precedence?
questions.explanation: In AWS IAM policy evaluation, an explicit Deny always overrides any Allow. This is true regardless of where the Deny comes from — whether it is an SCP, a permission boundary, a resource policy, or an identity policy. Explicit deny is the highest priority in the evaluation logic.

options.a: The IAM Allow policy wins because it is closest to the user
options.b: The explicit Deny in the SCP always wins
options.c: Neither — the request is evaluated as implicitly denied
options.d: The SCP is only advisory and does not enforce permissions

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to assume an IAM role:

  aws sts assume-role --role-arn ___ --role-session-name test
questions.explanation: The --role-arn parameter requires the full ARN of the IAM role to assume. The format is arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT_ID:role/ROLE_NAME. The assume-role call returns temporary security credentials (access key, secret key, and session token) that you can use to make API calls with the role permissions.

questions.prompt: Match each IAM concept to its description.
questions.explanation: IAM policies define the permissions for identities. Trust policies on roles define which principals can assume the role. Permission boundaries set an upper limit on what permissions an IAM entity can have, regardless of its identity policies. SCPs are applied at the AWS Organization level to restrict what actions accounts in an OU can perform.
right: Controls what an identity CAN do
Controls who can ASSUME a role
Sets maximum permissions for an identity

questions.prompt:
You are setting up cross-account access. Account B's role has a trust
policy allowing Account A. When Account A tries to assume the role,
they get:

  An error occurred (AccessDenied) when calling the AssumeRole operation

The trust policy includes a condition requiring sts:ExternalId. Account A
is not passing an External ID. What is the fix?
questions.explanation: When a trust policy includes a condition for sts:ExternalId, the caller must include the matching --external-id parameter in the assume-role call. External ID is a security mechanism to prevent the confused deputy problem in cross-account access scenarios. Without the correct External ID the assume-role call is denied.

options.a: Remove the trust policy and recreate it
options.b: Add --external-id with the correct value to the assume-role call
options.c: Change the trust policy to use arn instead of account ID
options.d: Enable MFA on Account A's user

questions.prompt:
Complete the policy effect to deny an action:

  "Effect": "___"
questions.explanation: IAM policy statements require an Effect of either Allow or Deny. An explicit Deny always takes precedence over any Allow statement. This is a fundamental rule of IAM policy evaluation and cannot be overridden.

questions.prompt:
A Lambda function needs to read from an S3 bucket. You created an IAM
role with s3:GetObject permission and attached it to the Lambda. But
the Lambda still gets Access Denied on S3 calls. The role's trust
policy currently lists ec2.amazonaws.com as the trusted service.
What is wrong?
questions.explanation: For a Lambda function to use an IAM role as its execution role, the role trust policy must include lambda.amazonaws.com as an allowed principal. If the trust policy only allows ec2.amazonaws.com, the Lambda service cannot assume the role, and all API calls from the function will fail with Access Denied.

options.a: Lambda needs s3:ListBucket permission too
options.b: The trust policy must allow lambda.amazonaws.com as the trusted service
options.c: S3 bucket policy must explicitly allow the Lambda role
options.d: Lambda functions cannot use IAM roles

questions.prompt:
An IAM user has an identity policy that allows s3:* on all resources.
However, the user also has a permission boundary attached that only
allows s3:GetObject and s3:ListBucket. When the user tries
s3:PutObject, it is denied. Why?
questions.explanation: Effective permissions are the intersection of the identity-based policy and the permission boundary. Even though the identity policy allows s3:*, the permission boundary only allows s3:GetObject and s3:ListBucket. Since s3:PutObject is not in the permission boundary, the request is denied. Permission boundaries act as a maximum permissions cap.

options.a: The identity policy is malformed
options.b: There is an explicit Deny somewhere
options.c: Effective permissions are the intersection of identity policy and permission boundary
options.d: Permission boundaries only apply to federated users

questions.prompt:
Complete the principal in a trust policy to allow AWS Lambda to assume the role:

  "Service": "___"
questions.explanation: The Service principal for Lambda in a trust policy is lambda.amazonaws.com. This tells IAM that the AWS Lambda service is allowed to call sts:AssumeRole on this role. Each AWS service has its own service principal that follows the pattern service.amazonaws.com.

questions.prompt:
An IAM user can launch EC2 instances in us-east-1 but gets Access Denied
when trying to launch in us-west-2. The IAM policy allows ec2:RunInstances
on all resources. What is restricting the region?
questions.explanation: The aws:RequestedRegion condition key can be used in IAM policies to restrict which AWS regions an action can be performed in. Even though the Resource is set to *, a condition like "aws:RequestedRegion": "us-east-1" limits the action to that region only. This is a common pattern for enforcing regional compliance requirements.

options.a: EC2 is not available in us-west-2
options.b: A condition key aws:RequestedRegion in the policy restricts to us-east-1
options.c: The user needs a separate policy per region
options.d: VPC in us-west-2 does not exist

questions.prompt: Match each policy evaluation step to its role in the evaluation process.
questions.explanation: AWS evaluates policies in a specific order. First, any explicit Deny from any policy type immediately blocks the request. Then SCPs are checked at the organization level. Permission boundaries set the maximum allowed scope. Finally, identity-based policies determine what is actually permitted. A request must be allowed at every level and denied at none.
right: What is actually allowed for the identity

questions.prompt:
Your Lambda function processes data from an SQS queue. The function
needs about 10 seconds to complete, but it keeps timing out with a
"Task timed out after 3.00 seconds" error. You have not changed any
default settings. What needs to change?
questions.explanation: The default Lambda timeout is 3 seconds. If your function needs more time, you must increase the Timeout setting in the function configuration. The timeout can be set anywhere from 1 second to a maximum of 900 seconds (15 minutes). For this case, setting it to at least 15 seconds would provide a safe margin above the 10 seconds needed.

options.a: Increase the Lambda memory allocation
options.b: Increase the Lambda timeout setting beyond 3 seconds
options.c: Switch from SQS to SNS as the event source
options.d: Enable provisioned concurrency

questions.prompt:
Complete the SAM template to set a Lambda function timeout to 30 seconds:

  Timeout: ___
questions.explanation: In a SAM or CloudFormation template, the Timeout property is specified in seconds as an integer value. Setting Timeout to 30 gives the function 30 seconds to complete execution before Lambda terminates it. Always set the timeout higher than your expected execution time to account for variability.

questions.prompt: Match each Lambda event source to its invocation type.
questions.explanation: API Gateway invokes Lambda synchronously — it waits for the response. S3 and SNS invoke Lambda asynchronously — they fire and forget, with Lambda handling retries. SQS uses polling-based invocation where the Lambda service polls the queue for messages and invokes your function with batches of messages.

questions.prompt:
Your Lambda function is deployed in a VPC private subnet. It needs to
call an external API on the internet but the requests time out.
The function has correct IAM permissions and the security group
allows all outbound traffic. What is missing?
questions.explanation: Lambda functions in a VPC private subnet do not have direct internet access. To reach the internet, you need a NAT Gateway deployed in a public subnet and a route in the private subnet route table pointing 0.0.0.0/0 to the NAT Gateway. Without this, all outbound internet requests from the Lambda function will time out.

options.a: A VPC endpoint for Lambda
options.b: A NAT Gateway in a public subnet with a route from the private subnet
options.c: An Internet Gateway attached directly to the private subnet
options.d: An Elastic IP assigned to the Lambda function

questions.prompt:
What is the maximum timeout value for an AWS Lambda function in seconds?

  Timeout: ___
questions.explanation: The maximum Lambda timeout is 900 seconds (15 minutes). This is a hard limit that cannot be increased. If your workload requires more than 15 minutes of execution time, you need to consider alternatives such as AWS Step Functions, AWS Fargate, or breaking the work into smaller chunks.

questions.prompt:
Your Java-based Lambda function has cold starts taking over 5 seconds.
The function is configured with 128 MB of memory. Users are complaining
about slow response times on the first request. What is the best fix?
questions.explanation: Lambda allocates CPU power proportional to memory. At 128 MB, the function gets minimal CPU. Increasing memory to 512 MB or higher gives more CPU, which significantly reduces cold start times especially for Java. Other strategies include using provisioned concurrency, GraalVM native images, or SnapStart for Java, but increasing memory is the simplest first step.

options.a: Switch to Python runtime
options.b: Increase memory allocation which also increases CPU proportionally
options.c: Decrease the timeout to force faster execution
options.d: Disable CloudWatch logging

questions.prompt:
Your Lambda function processes messages from an SQS queue. The function
runs successfully and returns 200, but the same messages keep reappearing
in the queue and getting processed again. The Lambda timeout is 60 seconds
and the SQS visibility timeout is 30 seconds. What is the problem?
questions.explanation: When SQS visibility timeout (30s) is less than the Lambda timeout (60s), messages can become visible again in the queue while the Lambda is still processing them. This causes duplicate processing. The fix is to set the SQS visibility timeout to at least 6 times the Lambda function timeout as recommended by AWS. In this case, set it to at least 360 seconds.

options.a: The Lambda function is not deleting the messages
options.b: The SQS visibility timeout is shorter than the Lambda timeout
options.c: The DLQ is sending messages back to the main queue
options.d: The Lambda event source mapping batch size is too large

questions.prompt:
Complete the minimum memory allocation for a Lambda function:

  minimum ___ MB to maximum 10240 MB
questions.explanation: Lambda memory can be configured between 128 MB and 10240 MB (10 GB) in 1 MB increments. Memory allocation directly affects CPU allocation — at 1769 MB a function gets one full vCPU. Choosing the right memory size is important for both performance and cost optimization.

questions.prompt:
You run the following command and get "Access Denied":

  awslocal s3api put-object --bucket my-bucket --key file.txt --body file.txt

The bucket policy requires server-side encryption for all uploads.
What flag is missing from the command?
questions.explanation: When a bucket policy includes a condition requiring server-side encryption (s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption), every put-object request must include the --server-side-encryption flag. Without it, S3 rejects the upload with a 403 Access Denied error. The correct flag is --server-side-encryption AES256.

options.a: --acl public-read
options.b: --server-side-encryption AES256
options.c: --content-type application/octet-stream
options.d: --storage-class STANDARD

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to list all S3 buckets:

  awslocal s3api ___
questions.explanation: The s3api list-buckets command returns a list of all buckets owned by the authenticated sender of the request. This is one of the most basic S3 API operations and requires s3:ListAllMyBuckets permission.

questions.prompt: Match each S3 encryption type to how its encryption key is managed.
questions.explanation: SSE-S3 uses an AES-256 key fully managed by AWS. SSE-KMS uses AWS Key Management Service, giving you an audit trail via CloudTrail. SSE-C requires you to send the encryption key with each request. Client-side encryption means the data is encrypted before it ever reaches S3.
right: AWS KMS manages key with audit trail
Customer provides key in every request

questions.prompt:
You created a bucket in us-west-2 but when you run:

  aws s3 ls

from a terminal configured for us-east-1, the bucket does not appear.
What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: S3 bucket names are globally unique and s3 ls should eventually show all buckets regardless of region. However, newly created buckets may experience a brief eventual consistency delay before they appear in list operations. If the bucket was just created, waiting a moment and retrying usually resolves it.

options.a: S3 buckets are region-specific and never appear cross-region
options.b: The IAM user does not have s3:ListAllMyBuckets permission
options.c: Eventual consistency delay — the bucket was just created
options.d: You need to specify --region us-west-2 in the list command

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to enable versioning on a bucket:

  awslocal s3api put-bucket-versioning --bucket my-bucket --versioning-configuration ___
questions.explanation: The --versioning-configuration parameter takes a shorthand syntax of Status=Enabled to turn on versioning. Once enabled, S3 keeps all versions of an object including all writes and even deletes. Versioning can be suspended but never fully disabled once enabled.

questions.prompt:
You generated a pre-signed URL for an S3 object and shared it with a
colleague. They get a 403 Forbidden error when clicking the link.
You test it yourself and also get 403. The object exists and your IAM
user has full S3 access. The URL was generated 2 days ago.
What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Pre-signed URLs have an expiration time (default is often 1 hour for STS credentials, up to 7 days for IAM user credentials). After expiration, the URL returns 403 Forbidden. The fix is to generate a new pre-signed URL with a longer --expires-in value appropriate for your use case.

options.a: Pre-signed URLs can only be used by the account that generated them
options.b: The pre-signed URL has expired
options.c: The object ACL blocks public access
options.d: The bucket has MFA Delete enabled

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to copy a local file to an S3 bucket:

  awslocal s3 cp ./file.txt ___
questions.explanation: The s3 cp command copies files between local filesystem and S3. The destination must use the s3:// URI scheme followed by the bucket name and object key path. For example, s3://my-bucket/file.txt uploads the file with the key file.txt in my-bucket.

questions.prompt:
You deleted several objects from a bucket, but when you list the bucket
contents they still appear. Running get-object on them returns a
404 error though. What feature is causing this behavior?
questions.explanation: When versioning is enabled, a DELETE operation does not permanently remove an object. Instead, S3 inserts a delete marker which becomes the current version. The object appears in listings that include versions but a standard GET returns 404. To permanently delete, you must delete the specific version ID or remove all versions including delete markers.

options.a: S3 Replication is restoring the objects
options.b: Versioning is enabled — delete creates markers, not actual deletes
options.c: S3 lifecycle rules are preventing deletion
options.d: The bucket has Object Lock enabled

questions.prompt:
A Lambda function processes messages from an SQS queue. The function
completes successfully but the same messages keep reappearing in the
queue. The Lambda timeout is 120 seconds and the SQS visibility
timeout is 30 seconds. What is the problem?
questions.explanation: When the SQS visibility timeout (30s) is shorter than the Lambda function timeout (120s), messages become visible in the queue again while Lambda is still processing them. This causes duplicate processing. AWS recommends setting the visibility timeout to at least 6 times the Lambda function timeout. In this case, the visibility timeout should be at least 720 seconds.

options.a: The Lambda function is not returning a success response
options.b: The visibility timeout is less than the Lambda timeout so messages become visible again during processing
options.c: The DLQ maxReceiveCount is set to 1
options.d: The SQS queue is a FIFO queue causing redelivery

questions.prompt:
Complete the redrive policy to send messages to a DLQ after 3 failed
processing attempts:

  maxReceiveCount: ___
questions.explanation: The maxReceiveCount in a redrive policy specifies how many times a message can be received from the queue before it is moved to the dead-letter queue. A value of 3 means after 3 failed processing attempts, the message is sent to the DLQ for investigation. Common values are 3 to 5 depending on the retry strategy and error tolerance.

questions.prompt: Match each SQS concept to its delivery/behavior characteristic.
questions.explanation: Standard queues offer maximum throughput with at-least-once delivery but may deliver duplicates. FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once processing and maintain strict message ordering. Dead-letter queues store messages that exceed the maxReceiveCount to prevent poison pill scenarios. Delay queues postpone delivery of new messages for a configured number of seconds.
right: Storage for messages that failed processing
Messages are invisible for a configured delay period

questions.prompt:
You created a FIFO SQS queue and your producer application is sending
messages. However, the SendMessage API call fails with:

  MissingParameter: The request must contain the parameter MessageGroupId

What is the fix?
questions.explanation: FIFO queues require a MessageGroupId for every message. This ID determines the message group — messages within the same group are processed in strict FIFO order. Different groups can be processed in parallel. You must include this parameter in every SendMessage or SendMessageBatch call. Additionally, FIFO queues require a MessageDeduplicationId or content-based deduplication.

options.a: Convert the queue to a standard queue
options.b: Add a MessageGroupId parameter to every SendMessage call
options.c: Set a default MessageGroupId on the queue configuration
options.d: Use SendMessageBatch instead of SendMessage

questions.prompt:
Complete the required name suffix for an SQS FIFO queue:

  my-queue___
questions.explanation: SQS FIFO queue names must end with the .fifo suffix. This is a hard requirement — the API will reject queue creation if the name does not end with .fifo when FifoQueue is set to true. For example, a valid FIFO queue name would be orders-queue.fifo.

questions.prompt:
Your application consumes messages from a standard SQS queue. Occasionally
it processes the same message twice, causing duplicate records in the
database. Is this a bug in SQS?
questions.explanation: Standard SQS queues provide at-least-once delivery, which means a message can occasionally be delivered more than once. This is by design for maximum throughput and availability. To prevent duplicates, either make your consumer idempotent (recommended), use a FIFO queue for exactly-once processing, or implement a deduplication mechanism in your application using a unique message identifier.

options.a: Yes — SQS should never deliver a message more than once
options.b: No — standard queues guarantee at-least-once delivery so duplicates are expected
options.c: Yes — this only happens if the visibility timeout is misconfigured
options.d: No — but only because you are using long polling

questions.prompt:
An EC2 instance in a public subnet has a public IP address. The security
group allows all outbound traffic. However, the instance cannot reach
the internet. You check the route table associated with the subnet.
What is most likely missing?
questions.explanation: A public subnet is defined by having a route to an Internet Gateway. Even if the instance has a public IP and the security group allows outbound traffic, without a 0.0.0.0/0 route pointing to an IGW in the route table, the instance cannot send or receive traffic from the internet.

options.a: An Elastic IP address
options.b: A route for 0.0.0.0/0 pointing to the Internet Gateway
options.c: A NAT Gateway in the same subnet
options.d: DNS resolution enabled on the VPC

questions.prompt:
Complete the CIDR block for a /24 subnet starting at 10.0.1:

  10.0.1.___
questions.explanation: A /24 CIDR block provides 256 IP addresses (10.0.1.0 through 10.0.1.255). AWS reserves 5 addresses in each subnet (first four and last one), leaving 251 usable addresses. The /24 prefix length means the first 24 bits are the network portion, and the last 8 bits are for host addresses.

questions.prompt: Match each VPC component to its description.
questions.explanation: Security Groups are stateful — return traffic is automatically allowed. NACLs are stateless — you must define both inbound and outbound rules. Route Tables contain rules that determine where traffic is directed. Internet Gateways provide a target for internet-routable traffic and perform NAT for instances with public IP addresses.
right: Enables internet connectivity for the VPC

questions.prompt:
An EC2 instance in a private subnet needs to reach the internet to
download packages. A NAT Gateway exists in a public subnet. The instance
still cannot reach the internet. What should you check first?
questions.explanation: For a private subnet to use a NAT Gateway, the private subnet's route table must have a route for 0.0.0.0/0 pointing to the NAT Gateway ID. The NAT Gateway itself must be in a public subnet (one with a route to an IGW). NAT Gateways do not have security groups — they use NACLs of their subnet. The instance does not need a public IP.

options.a: The instance needs a public IP address
options.b: The private subnet route table needs a 0.0.0.0/0 route to the NAT Gateway
options.c: The NAT Gateway needs a security group allowing outbound traffic
options.d: The IGW must be in the same subnet as the instance

questions.prompt:
You can SSH (port 22) into your EC2 instance, but when you try to
access the web application running on port 80, the connection is
refused. The web server is confirmed running on the instance.
What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Security Groups are deny-by-default for inbound traffic. If only port 22 is allowed inbound, port 80 connections will be refused. The fix is to add an inbound rule allowing TCP port 80 (and/or 443 for HTTPS) from the desired source CIDR. Since SSH works, the network path and route table are functioning correctly.

options.a: The route table is blocking port 80
options.b: The security group only allows inbound port 22 and needs a rule for port 80
options.c: The NACL is blocking port 80 outbound
options.d: The IGW only forwards SSH traffic

questions.prompt:
Complete the AWS CLI flag to allow all outbound traffic protocols in a
security group rule:

  --protocol ___ --port all
questions.explanation: In AWS security group rules, protocol -1 means all protocols (TCP, UDP, ICMP, etc.). When you specify --protocol -1, the --port parameter should be set to all, allowing all ports for all protocols. This is typically used for outbound rules where you want unrestricted egress traffic.

questions.prompt:
You configured a NACL on a public subnet to allow inbound HTTP (port 80).
External clients can send requests to your web server, but they never
receive responses. The security group allows both inbound 80 and all
outbound. What is the issue?
questions.explanation: NACLs are stateless, meaning return traffic must be explicitly allowed. When a client connects to port 80, the response goes back on an ephemeral port (1024-65535). Without an outbound NACL rule allowing these ports, the response packets are dropped. Security Groups are stateful and handle this automatically, but NACLs require both directions to be configured.

options.a: The IGW is not performing NAT correctly
options.b: The NACL is stateless and needs an outbound rule for ephemeral ports 1024-65535
options.c: The route table is missing a return route
options.d: The security group outbound rule is overridden by the NACL

questions.prompt:
Complete the AWS CLI command to add a route to the internet via an
Internet Gateway:

  aws ec2 create-route --route-table-id rtb-xxx --destination-cidr-block 0.0.0.0/0 --gateway-id ___
questions.explanation: The --gateway-id parameter takes the ID of an Internet Gateway, which follows the format igw- followed by a unique identifier. This route sends all traffic destined for addresses outside the VPC (0.0.0.0/0) through the Internet Gateway, making the subnet a public subnet.

questions.prompt: Match each subnet type to its defining route table characteristic.
questions.explanation: A public subnet has a route table entry pointing 0.0.0.0/0 to an IGW. A private subnet typically routes 0.0.0.0/0 to a NAT Gateway for outbound internet access without exposing instances directly. A VPN-only subnet routes traffic through a Virtual Private Gateway (VGW) which connects to an on-premises network via VPN or Direct Connect.
right: Has a route to an Internet Gateway
Has a route to a NAT Gateway
Has a route to a Virtual Private Gateway

questions.prompt:
You established a VPC peering connection between VPC-A (10.0.0.0/16)
and VPC-B (10.1.0.0/16). The peering connection status shows "Active".
However, instances in VPC-A cannot ping instances in VPC-B.
Security groups in both VPCs allow ICMP from all sources.
What is missing?
questions.explanation: An active VPC peering connection alone does not route traffic. You must manually add routes in each VPC route table. VPC-A needs a route for 10.1.0.0/16 pointing to the peering connection ID, and VPC-B needs a route for 10.0.0.0/16 pointing to the same peering connection. Without these routes, instances have no path to reach the peered VPC.

options.a: VPC peering does not support ICMP traffic
options.b: Route tables in both VPCs need routes to each other's CIDR via the peering connection
options.c: DNS resolution is not enabled on the peering connection
options.d: You need a transit gateway instead of peering

quizzes.topic: GitLab CI/CD YAML Quoting and Special Characters

questions.prompt:
A pipeline fails with a YAML parse error on this line:

script: echo {test}

The error indicates an unexpected mapping. What is the problem and how
should it be fixed?
questions.explanation: YAML interprets curly braces as flow mapping syntax. When the YAML parser encounters {test}, it tries to parse it as a mapping and fails. Wrapping the value in quotes prevents this interpretation. Both single quotes (script: 'echo {test}') and double quotes (script: "echo {test}") work. Other special characters that require quoting include colons, hash signs, square brackets, and exclamation marks.

options.a: The echo command is not available in the runner image
options.b: Curly braces are interpreted as a YAML mapping; quote the string with double or single quotes
options.c: The script keyword requires an array, not a single string
options.d: The word test is a YAML reserved keyword

questions.prompt:
Which quoting style prevents variable expansion in GitLab CI YAML?

To preserve a literal $VAR without expansion, use ___ quotes.
questions.explanation: Single quotes in YAML preserve the literal string content without any interpretation. A value like '$MY_VAR' will remain as the literal text $MY_VAR. Double quotes allow escape sequences and, in GitLab CI context, variables within double-quoted strings are expanded by the shell during script execution. Use single quotes when you need the exact literal value.

questions.prompt: Match each YAML quoting or block scalar style to its behavior.
questions.explanation: Single quotes preserve content literally with no special character processing. Double quotes allow escape sequences and variable expansion in shell contexts. The pipe (|) block scalar preserves newlines exactly as written, making it ideal for multi-line scripts. The greater-than (>) folded scalar joins consecutive lines with spaces, useful for long explanations or descriptions that should flow as a single paragraph.

questions.prompt:
A pipeline fails with a YAML parse error on this rules configuration:

rules:
  - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == "main"

The error points to the line containing if: and mentions an unexpected
mapping value. What is the problem and how should it be fixed?
questions.explanation: In YAML, a colon followed by a space is the mapping indicator. The parser sees if: as a mapping key and then tries to parse the rest as its value, which conflicts with the outer mapping structure. The fix is to quote the entire condition string, for example: if: '$CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == "main"'. This tells the parser to treat everything after if: as a single string value.

options.a: The variable CI_COMMIT_BRANCH does not exist in this context
options.b: The colon after if makes the YAML parser interpret it as a mapping key; quote the entire value
options.c: The double equals operator is not supported in rules
options.d: The rules keyword must come before the script keyword

questions.prompt:
Complete the block scalar indicator to preserve newlines in a multi-line
script:

script: ___
  echo "step 1"
  echo "step 2"
  echo "step 3"
questions.explanation: The pipe character (|) introduces a literal block scalar in YAML. It preserves newlines exactly as written, so each echo command will be on its own line. The variant |- strips the trailing newline. In GitLab CI, multi-line scripts using | are passed to the shell with newlines intact, allowing each line to execute as a separate command. The alternative > would fold all lines into one, which is not what you want for scripts.

questions.prompt:
A developer writes the following script line in their .gitlab-ci.yml:

script:
  - echo 'Version is $BUILD_VERSION'

The job runs successfully but prints "Version is $BUILD_VERSION" literally
instead of the variable's value. What is the cause and the fix?
questions.explanation: In shell execution, single quotes preserve everything literally. The string $BUILD_VERSION is passed to echo as-is without expansion. To have the shell substitute the variable's value, use double quotes instead: echo "Version is $BUILD_VERSION". This applies both to the shell behavior at runtime and is consistent with how YAML single quotes prevent special character interpretation at the parsing level.

options.a: The variable BUILD_VERSION is not defined in the project settings
options.b: Single quotes prevent variable expansion; use double quotes to allow the shell to expand the variable
options.c: The echo command does not support variable interpolation
options.d: Variables can only be used in the variables section, not in scripts

questions.prompt:
A rolling update is stuck and no new pods are being created. The
deployment strategy has maxUnavailable set to 0 and maxSurge set to 0.
The desired replica count is 3 and 3 old pods are running. What is the
problem?
questions.explanation: With maxUnavailable 0, Kubernetes cannot remove any old pods. With maxSurge 0, it cannot create any new pods above the desired count. This creates a deadlock where the rollout cannot proceed. At least one of these values must be greater than 0 to allow the rolling update to make progress.

options.a: The deployment needs more replicas to perform a rolling update
options.b: At least one of maxUnavailable or maxSurge must be greater than 0
options.c: Rolling updates require a Recreate strategy to work properly
options.d: The old pods need to be manually deleted before the update proceeds

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to rollback a deployment to the previous revision:
kubectl rollout ___ deployment/my-app
questions.explanation: kubectl rollout undo reverts a deployment to its previous revision. You can specify a particular revision with --to-revision=N. Kubernetes keeps rollout history based on the revisionHistoryLimit field (default 10), allowing you to roll back to any stored revision.

questions.prompt: Match each probe or hook with its primary purpose.
questions.explanation: Liveness probes detect deadlocked or hung containers and trigger a restart. Readiness probes control whether a pod receives traffic by adding or removing it from service endpoints. Startup probes protect slow-starting containers by disabling liveness and readiness checks until the app is ready. The preStop hook runs a command before the container receives SIGTERM, useful for graceful shutdown preparation.
right: Delays other probes during slow startup

questions.prompt:
The HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) is configured for a deployment but
shows "unknown" for current CPU utilization and never scales. Running
kubectl top pods returns "error: Metrics API not available". What is the
cause?
questions.explanation: HPA relies on the Metrics API to read current CPU and memory usage. The metrics-server component collects resource metrics from kubelets and exposes them via the Metrics API. Without it, HPA cannot determine current utilization and will not scale. Install metrics-server with kubectl apply or via Helm.

options.a: The pods do not have CPU limits configured
options.b: The HPA targetCPUUtilizationPercentage is set too high
options.c: The metrics-server is not installed in the cluster
options.d: The deployment needs at least 3 replicas for HPA to work

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to check the status of a rolling update:
kubectl rollout ___ deployment/my-app
questions.explanation: kubectl rollout status watches a deployment rollout and reports its progress. It exits with code 0 when the rollout completes successfully or a non-zero code if it fails or times out. This is commonly used in CI/CD pipelines to verify that a deployment succeeded.

questions.prompt:
A new version is deployed and new pods are crashing immediately with an
application error. However, the rollout continues and eventually all old
pods are replaced with crashing new pods. The deployment has no readiness
probe configured. Why did the rollout not stop?
questions.explanation: Without a readiness probe, Kubernetes marks a pod as ready as soon as all containers start, even if they crash shortly after. This means the rolling update sees new pods as "ready" and continues replacing old pods. Adding a readiness probe ensures pods are only marked ready when the application can actually serve traffic, which would pause the rollout when new pods fail their health checks.

options.a: The deployment progressDeadlineSeconds is too high
options.b: Without a readiness probe, Kubernetes considers all pods ready as soon as they start
options.c: The minReadySeconds value is set to 0
options.d: The deployment revision history limit is too low

questions.prompt:
HPA is configured with targetCPUUtilizationPercentage of 50% and keeps
scaling to the maximum replica count. The pods have no CPU resource
requests defined. Actual CPU usage per pod is low (about 10m). What is
causing the excessive scaling?
questions.explanation: HPA calculates CPU utilization as a percentage of the CPU request. If no CPU request is set, HPA cannot compute the percentage correctly and may report unknown or extreme values, causing it to scale to the maximum. Always define CPU requests on pods when using HPA so it has a baseline for calculating utilization.

options.a: The target percentage should be set higher
options.b: HPA needs CPU requests on pods to calculate utilization percentage
options.c: The metrics-server is reporting incorrect values
options.d: The minimum replica count is set too low

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to set up HPA with a CPU utilization target:
kubectl autoscale deployment my-app --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=___
questions.explanation: The --cpu-percent flag sets the target average CPU utilization across all pods. Common values are 50-80 percent. At 80 percent, HPA will add replicas when average CPU usage exceeds 80 percent of the requested CPU and remove replicas when it drops below. Choose a value that balances responsiveness with cost efficiency.

questions.prompt:
A pod keeps restarting with status OOMKilled. The container has a memory
limit of 64Mi, but the application typically requires around 256Mi to run.
What should you do?
questions.explanation: OOMKilled means the container exceeded its memory limit and was killed by the kernel OOM killer. The fix is to increase the memory limit (and request) so the container has enough memory to operate. Setting it to at least 256Mi matches what the application needs.

options.a: Delete the pod and let the controller recreate it
options.b: Increase the container memory limit to at least 256Mi
options.c: Add a liveness probe to restart the container faster
options.d: Change the restart policy to Never

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to view the logs of a pod:
kubectl ___ my-pod
questions.explanation: kubectl logs is the command to retrieve container logs from a pod. You can add -f to follow the log stream or -p to see logs from a previous (crashed) container instance.

questions.prompt: Match each container exit code with its meaning.
questions.explanation: Exit code 0 means the process completed successfully. Exit code 1 is a general application error. Exit code 137 (128+9) means the process received SIGKILL, typically from the OOM killer. Exit code 143 (128+15) means the process received SIGTERM, the graceful shutdown signal sent during pod termination.

questions.prompt:
A pod is in CrashLoopBackOff. The logs show:
"Error: connect ECONNREFUSED 10.96.0.15:5432 - connection refused to
database". The database is supposed to be running in the same namespace.
What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Connection refused means the target address is reachable but nothing is listening on that port. The database pod or service is either not running, not ready, or the connection string points to the wrong service endpoint. Check that the database pod is running and the service selectors match.

options.a: The pod needs more CPU resources
options.b: The database service is not running or has a wrong endpoint
options.c: The pod image is corrupted and needs to be rebuilt
options.d: The namespace has a NetworkPolicy blocking all traffic

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to get detailed information about a pod:
kubectl ___ pod my-pod
questions.explanation: kubectl describe pod shows detailed information including events, conditions, container statuses, volumes, and more. It is one of the most useful commands for troubleshooting pod issues.

questions.prompt:
A pod is stuck in ImagePullBackOff. Running kubectl describe shows:
"Failed to pull image nginx:lates - manifest unknown". You notice the
image tag is set to "lates". What should you do?
questions.explanation: ImagePullBackOff occurs when Kubernetes cannot pull the container image. In this case the image tag "lates" is a typo and should be "latest" or whatever the correct tag is. The error message "manifest unknown" confirms that the specified tag does not exist in the registry.

options.a: Delete the pod and recreate it with the same manifest
options.b: Switch to a private registry since Docker Hub is down
options.c: Fix the image tag typo from "lates" to "latest" or the correct tag
options.d: Add imagePullSecrets to the pod spec

questions.prompt:
A pod is stuck in ContainerCreating for several minutes. Events show:
"persistentvolumeclaim my-data-pvc not found". The pod spec references a
volume claim named my-data-pvc. What should you do?
questions.explanation: ContainerCreating with a PVC not found error means the pod cannot start because the referenced PersistentVolumeClaim does not exist. You need to either create the PVC with the name my-data-pvc or correct the volume claim name in the pod specification to reference an existing PVC.

options.a: Restart the kubelet on the node
options.b: Increase the pod resource requests
options.c: Create the missing PVC or fix the volume claim name in the pod spec
options.d: Delete the pod and let it be rescheduled to another node

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to list cluster events:
kubectl get ___
questions.explanation: kubectl get events lists all events in the current namespace. Events provide valuable troubleshooting information such as scheduling decisions, image pull results, and container state changes. Use --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp to see them in chronological order.

questions.prompt:
A pod is Running but the service reports it as not ready. The application
starts and listens on port 8080, but the readiness probe is configured
to check port 80. What is the problem?
questions.explanation: The readiness probe determines whether the pod should receive traffic. If the probe checks port 80 but the application listens on 8080, the probe will always fail and the pod will never be marked ready. Fix the readiness probe to check port 8080 where the application is actually listening.

options.a: The pod needs a liveness probe instead of a readiness probe
options.b: The service port configuration is wrong
options.c: The readiness probe port must be changed from 80 to 8080
options.d: The container needs to expose port 80 explicitly

questions.prompt: Match each pod status with its meaning.
questions.explanation: Pending means the pod has been accepted but is waiting for scheduling or volume binding. Running means all containers have been created and at least one is running. CrashLoopBackOff means a container keeps crashing and Kubernetes is applying exponential backoff before restarting. ImagePullBackOff means Kubernetes cannot pull the specified container image from the registry.

questions.prompt:
A pod using ServiceAccount my-sa tries to list pods in the default
namespace but receives: "Error: pods is forbidden: User
system:serviceaccount:default:my-sa cannot list resource pods". A Role
called pod-reader exists with the correct permissions. What is missing?
questions.explanation: A Role defines permissions but does not grant them to anyone. You need a RoleBinding to bind the Role to a subject such as a ServiceAccount, user, or group. Without the binding, the ServiceAccount has no permissions beyond the defaults.

options.a: The Role needs additional verbs like get and watch
options.b: A RoleBinding linking the pod-reader Role to the my-sa ServiceAccount
options.c: The ServiceAccount must be recreated with special annotations
options.d: A ClusterRole is required because Roles cannot grant pod list access

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to create a RoleBinding that binds the pod-reader
Role to a ServiceAccount:
kubectl create rolebinding my-binding --role=pod-reader --serviceaccount=___
questions.explanation: The --serviceaccount flag uses the format namespace:name. For a ServiceAccount called my-sa in the default namespace, you write default:my-sa. This binds the pod-reader Role to that specific ServiceAccount within the RoleBinding namespace.

questions.prompt: Match each RBAC resource with its scope and purpose.
questions.explanation: Role and RoleBinding are namespaced resources. A Role defines permissions within a namespace and a RoleBinding grants those permissions to subjects in that namespace. ClusterRole and ClusterRoleBinding are cluster-scoped. A ClusterRole defines permissions across all namespaces and a ClusterRoleBinding grants them globally.
right: Binds role to subject in a namespace
Binds cluster role to subject globally

questions.prompt:
A ClusterRole grants list permissions for pods. A developer can list
pods in the default namespace but gets forbidden in kube-system. You
used a RoleBinding (not ClusterRoleBinding) to bind the ClusterRole.
What is the issue?
questions.explanation: A RoleBinding can reference a ClusterRole, but it only grants the permissions within the namespace where the RoleBinding exists. To grant access across all namespaces, you must use a ClusterRoleBinding. This is a common mistake when setting up RBAC permissions.

options.a: ClusterRoles cannot be used with RoleBindings
options.b: RoleBinding only grants access in its own namespace; use ClusterRoleBinding for all namespaces
options.c: The kube-system namespace has special protections that prevent all access
options.d: The developer needs a separate ClusterRole for system namespaces

questions.prompt:
Complete the missing verb to allow full read access to pods:
verbs: ["get", "list", "___"]
questions.explanation: The three standard read verbs in Kubernetes RBAC are get (single resource), list (collection), and watch (stream changes). Together they provide complete read access. Without watch, controllers and informers cannot receive real-time updates about resource changes.

questions.prompt:
A pod can successfully list pods (core API group) but gets forbidden
when trying to list deployments. The Role has this rule:
apiGroups: [""]
resources: ["pods", "deployments"]
verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
What is wrong?
questions.explanation: Pods are in the core API group (represented by ""), but deployments are in the "apps" API group. A single rule with apiGroups [""] only covers core resources. You need a separate rule with apiGroups ["apps"] for deployments, or add "apps" as an additional entry if structuring rules to cover multiple API groups.

options.a: The verbs are incorrect for deployments
options.b: Deployments require a ClusterRole, not a Role
options.c: Deployments are in the "apps" apiGroup, not the core "" apiGroup
options.d: The resources field cannot contain both pods and deployments

questions.prompt:
A Service exists but kubectl get endpoints shows no endpoints. The pod
has labels app=web and tier=frontend, but the service selector is set to
app=web and tier=backend. What is the problem?
questions.explanation: A service selects pods using label selectors. All selector labels must match for a pod to be included as an endpoint. Here tier=backend on the service does not match tier=frontend on the pod. Fix the selector to match the actual pod labels so the service can route traffic correctly.

options.a: The pod is not in the Ready state
options.b: The service selector labels do not match the pod labels
options.c: The service is of type ExternalName and does not use selectors
options.d: The endpoints controller is not running

questions.prompt:
Complete the full DNS name for a service called my-service in the
default namespace:
my-service.___
questions.explanation: Kubernetes DNS follows the pattern service-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local. For a service called my-service in the default namespace, the full DNS name is my-service.default.svc.cluster.local. Within the same namespace you can use just the service name.

questions.prompt: Match each Kubernetes service type with its behavior.
questions.explanation: ClusterIP is the default type providing an internal IP reachable only within the cluster. NodePort exposes the service on a static port on every node. LoadBalancer provisions an external load balancer from the cloud provider. ExternalName maps the service to a DNS CNAME record pointing to an external hostname.
right: DNS CNAME alias to external service

questions.prompt:
A NodePort service is accessible when curling the node IP and port from
inside the cluster, but external clients cannot connect. The cloud
provider firewall only allows ports 22, 80, and 443. What is the issue?
questions.explanation: NodePort services allocate a port in the range 30000-32767 by default. If the firewall only allows ports 22, 80, and 443, traffic to the NodePort will be blocked. You need to open the NodePort range in the firewall or use a LoadBalancer service type instead.

options.a: The service should be changed to ClusterIP type
options.b: The firewall does not allow the NodePort range (30000-32767)
options.c: The pod readiness probe is failing
options.d: kube-proxy is not running on the nodes

questions.prompt:
Complete the default NodePort range minimum value:
___-32767
questions.explanation: The default NodePort range in Kubernetes is 30000-32767. This can be customized using the --service-node-port-range flag on the API server, but the default range is what you will encounter in most clusters.

questions.prompt:
An Ingress resource is configured but all requests return HTTP 503
Service Unavailable. The backend service has port 80, but the container
in the target pods listens on port 3000. The service spec shows port 80
and targetPort 80. What needs to change?
questions.explanation: The service targetPort must match the port the container is actually listening on. Traffic flows from Ingress to service port to targetPort on the pod. If targetPort is 80 but the container listens on 3000, the connection fails with 503. Change targetPort to 3000 so traffic reaches the application correctly.

options.a: Change the Ingress backend port to 3000
options.b: Change the service targetPort from 80 to 3000 to match the container
options.c: Add an annotation to the Ingress for port rewriting
options.d: Change the container to listen on port 80

questions.prompt:
Complete the command to expose a deployment as a service:
kubectl expose deployment my-app --port=80 --type=___
questions.explanation: kubectl expose creates a service for a deployment. The --type flag sets the service type. ClusterIP is the default and provides internal-only access. NodePort adds external access via node ports. LoadBalancer provisions a cloud load balancer. Choose the type based on your access requirements.

questions.prompt:
A service is reachable using its ClusterIP address, but resolving the
service name fails with NXDOMAIN. Checking kube-system shows the
CoreDNS pod is in CrashLoopBackOff. What should you do?
questions.explanation: CoreDNS is the cluster DNS provider. When CoreDNS is down, pods cannot resolve service names to IP addresses. The fix is to diagnose and repair CoreDNS by checking its logs and configuration. Common issues include corrupted ConfigMaps, resource limits being too low, or upstream DNS server connectivity problems.

options.a: Restart all application pods to refresh DNS cache
options.b: Use IP addresses instead of DNS names for all services
options.c: Fix CoreDNS since DNS resolution requires a working CoreDNS deployment
options.d: Add hostAliases to all pods that need to resolve service names

questions.prompt:
A PersistentVolumeClaim is stuck in Pending status. The PVC requests 10Gi
of storage, but the only available PersistentVolume has 5Gi capacity and
no StorageClass is configured for dynamic provisioning. What should you do?
questions.explanation: A PVC binds to a PV that satisfies its requirements including capacity and access modes. If no PV has enough capacity and no StorageClass is available for dynamic provisioning, the PVC stays Pending. Either create a PV with sufficient capacity or set up a StorageClass with a provisioner for automatic volume creation.

options.a: Delete the PVC and recreate it with a smaller size
options.b: Create a PV with at least 10Gi capacity or configure a StorageClass for dynamic provisioning
options.c: Restart the kube-controller-manager to force binding
options.d: Change the PVC access mode to ReadWriteMany

questions.prompt:
Complete the access mode that allows read-write access from a single node:
accessModes: ["___"]
questions.explanation: ReadWriteOnce (RWO) allows the volume to be mounted as read-write by a single node. This is the most common access mode and is supported by most storage backends. Multiple pods on the same node can still access the volume simultaneously.

questions.prompt: Match each access mode abbreviation with its meaning.
questions.explanation: RWO (ReadWriteOnce) allows read-write on one node. ROX (ReadOnlyMany) allows read-only on many nodes. RWX (ReadWriteMany) allows read-write on many nodes and is needed for shared storage. RWOP (ReadWriteOncePod) is the strictest mode, allowing read-write by only a single pod, which was introduced in Kubernetes 1.22.

questions.prompt:
A PV has status Released after its PVC was deleted. A new PVC with the
same spec is created but stays Pending and does not bind to the existing
PV. The PV reclaim policy is Retain. What must be done?
questions.explanation: When a PV has reclaim policy Retain and its PVC is deleted, the PV enters Released state and keeps a claimRef to the old PVC. It will not bind to a new PVC until the claimRef is cleared. You can edit the PV to remove the claimRef field, which returns it to Available status, or create a new PV entirely.

options.a: Change the reclaim policy to Delete and recreate the PV
options.b: Delete the old PVC reference (claimRef) from the PV spec, or create a new PV
options.c: Restart the persistent volume controller
options.d: Set the PV status back to Available using kubectl patch

questions.prompt:
Complete the StorageClass name commonly used as the default:
storageClassName: "___"
questions.explanation: The default StorageClass name varies by environment. Minikube uses "standard", AWS EKS uses "gp2" or "gp3", GKE uses "standard" or "standard-rwo", and AKS uses "default". The default StorageClass is marked with the annotation storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class set to true.

questions.prompt:
A PVC is successfully bound to a PV, but the pod using it is stuck in
ContainerCreating. Events show "MountVolume.MountDevice failed: format
of disk /dev/xvdf failed". The StorageClass has fsType set to "ntfs"
on a Linux node. What is the issue?
questions.explanation: The fsType in the StorageClass determines how the volume is formatted. NTFS is a Windows filesystem and will fail to format on Linux nodes. Change the fsType to a Linux-compatible filesystem such as ext4 or xfs. Most StorageClasses default to ext4 if fsType is not specified.

options.a: The volume size is too small for the filesystem
options.b: The fsType in StorageClass is wrong; use a Linux-compatible type like ext4 or xfs
options.c: The node needs additional storage drivers installed
options.d: The PVC should request a different access mode

questions.prompt:
Two pods in the same deployment need to share data via a PVC. The PVC
uses access mode ReadWriteOnce. The first pod mounts successfully, but
the second pod fails with "Multi-Attach error for volume: Volume is
already attached to node-1". The pods are on different nodes. What
should you do?
questions.explanation: ReadWriteOnce only allows the volume to be mounted on a single node. If pods land on different nodes, the second pod cannot attach the volume. To share a volume across nodes, use ReadWriteMany (RWX) with a storage backend that supports it, such as NFS, EFS, or CephFS. Alternatively, use pod affinity to colocate pods on the same node.

options.a: Move both pods to the same node using nodeAffinity
options.b: Change the access mode to ReadWriteMany (RWX) with a compatible storage backend
options.c: Increase the PV size to allow multiple attachments
options.d: Add a sidecar container to handle file sharing

questions.prompt:
Complete the PV reclaim policy that automatically deletes the volume
when the PVC is released:
persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: ___
questions.explanation: The Delete policy removes the PV and its associated storage when the PVC is deleted. Retain keeps the PV and its data for manual cleanup. Recycle (deprecated) performed a basic rm -rf on the volume. Delete is the default for dynamically provisioned volumes and is appropriate when data does not need to survive PVC deletion.
